creative.concept: Comparison table — 'Mac dictation: the comparison nobody publishes.' Fresh format never used in campaign. Jot vs. SuperWhisper vs. Wispr Flow across 5 rows: Price, Audio leaves Mac, Account required, Works offline, Open source. The $0 vs. $249/yr vs. $180/yr is the scroll-stopper; 'The free one is the most private one.' is the punchline. No carousel (ran Jun 22 and Jun 25 — 2× this week already). Storyboard: inbox-clearing email-backlog use case.

shots.visual: Inbox with 47 unread emails; cursor hovering over unread subject lines
Click opens first email; cursor lands in the reply field
Option-Space pressed; Dynamic Island pill appears pulsing under Mac notch
Pill pulses with audio waveform; user speaks a 3-sentence reply naturally
Reply text fills the field; pill vanished; 'Send' button visible; jot-transcribe.com watermark

drafts.text:
Nobody publishes this comparison.

SuperWhisper: $249/yr. Wispr Flow: $180/yr. Jot: $0.

And: Jot is the only one that runs entirely on-device -- your audio never touches a server. No account required. Works offline. Open source.

The free one turns out to be the most private one.

71 days. 31 releases. 231 downloads.

Have you switched from a paid dictation app to a free one? What made you do it?

#macOS #indiedev #buildinpublic
drafts.note: Attach comparison-table.svg (export as 1080x1080 PNG). Do NOT put the link in the post body -- LinkedIn downranks posts with external links. Paste the utm_url as the FIRST COMMENT immediately after posting. Closing question about switching from paid is the engagement trigger.

drafts.text:
I made a comparison chart of Mac dictation apps because none of the roundups include the free one (I built it)

All the 'best Mac dictation' articles compare SuperWhisper, Wispr Flow, Whisper Transcription, Dictanote -- none of them include Jot, which is free and open source.

So here's the comparison they don't publish:

| | Jot | SuperWhisper | Wispr Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| Price | $0 | $249/yr | $180/yr |
| Audio leaves Mac | never | optional | always |
| Account required | no | no | yes |
| Works offline | always | always | never |
| Open source | yes | no | no |

Jot is free because dictation on a Mac shouldn't need a server. Transcription runs on the Apple Neural Engine -- works offline, nothing leaves your machine.

There's also AI cleanup, rewrite-with-voice, and custom vocabulary. All optional, all routable to Apple Intelligence on-device if you're on macOS 26.

Apple Silicon only, macOS 15+.

(I built this -- disclosure)

Link in comments.
drafts.note: Post to r/MacOS. recommended:false -- last recommended:true was Jun 23 (3 days ago); needs 4+ days -- earliest is Jun 27. Draft ready to post tomorrow. Answer every comment in the first hour.

opportunities.title: Best Offline Dictation Apps for Mac in 2026 -- Ammon Taylor (Medium)
opportunities.why: Offline dictation roundup missing the only fully-free open-source option. Medium comments are open to anyone and rank well in search. The 11-language, $0, on-device angle is directly on-topic for an offline roundup audience.
opportunities.suggested_reply: Great roundup. One worth adding: Jot (jot-transcribe.com) -- free, open-source, 11 languages on-device on the Apple Neural Engine. No subscription, no cloud, no account. Works offline by definition -- transcription runs entirely on your Mac. I built it. (Disclosure: developer.)

opportunities.title: 7 Best Offline Dictation Apps for Mac in 2026 -- Voibe
opportunities.why: High-SEO roundup targeting the same offline/privacy audience as today's comparison card. Jot not listed. Comment opportunity to add the only $0 open-source entry.
opportunities.suggested_reply: Add Jot to this list -- the only fully free, open-source on-device option. 11 languages as of v1.16, runs on the Apple Neural Engine, works offline by definition. No subscription, no cloud. I built it. (Disclosure: developer.)
